§8.4 Density and Centre of Mass

Density measures the amount of some quantity per unit size.
e.g. kg / m 3 , lb / ft kg / m 3 , lb / ft kg//m^(3),lb//ft\mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}, \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}kg/m3,lb/ft, people/square mile.
To find the total amount of some quantity from the density:
  1. Divide the region into small pieces on each of which the density is approximately constant.
The amount for each piece is approximately the density (somewhere in the piece) multiplied by the size of the piece.
2. Add up all the contributions for all the pieces.
This gives us a Riemann sum which approximates the total amount.
3. Let the number of pieces tend to infinity in a suitable way.
This gives us an integral which we define to be the total amount.

Example

A 6 ft long rod lies on the x x xxx-axis with its left end at x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0. If the linear density of the rod is
λ ( x ) = 1 + x 2 6 lb / ft , λ ( x ) = 1 + x 2 6 lb / ft , lambda(x)=1+(x^(2))/(6)lb//ft,\lambda(x)=1+\frac{x^{2}}{6} \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft},λ(x)=1+x26lb/ft,
find the mass of the rod.
The mass of a small piece is
λ ( x ) Δ x = ( 1 + x 2 6 ) Δ x . λ ( x ) Δ x = 1 + x 2 6 Δ x . lambda(x)Delta x=(1+(x^(2))/(6))Delta x.\lambda(x) \Delta x=\left(1+\frac{x^{2}}{6}\right) \Delta x .λ(x)Δx=(1+x26)Δx.
If we add together the masses of the small pieces, we get
M ≈ ∑ i = 0 n − 1 λ ( x i − 1 ) Δ x = ∑ i = 0 n − 1 ( 1 + x i 2 6 ) Δ x . M ≈ ∑ i = 0 n − 1   λ x i − 1 Δ x = ∑ i = 0 n − 1   1 + x i 2 6 Δ x . M~~sum_(i=0)^(n-1)lambda(x_(i-1))Delta x=sum_(i=0)^(n-1)(1+(x_(i)^(2))/(6))Delta x.M \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \lambda\left(x_{i-1}\right) \Delta x=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\left(1+\frac{x_{i}^{2}}{6}\right) \Delta x .M≈∑i=0n−1λ(xi−1)Δx=∑i=0n−1(1+xi26)Δx.
Letting n → ∞ n → ∞ n rarr oon \rightarrow \inftyn→∞, we get
M = ∫ 0 6 λ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 6 ( 1 + x 2 6 ) d x = [ x + x 3 18 ] 0 6 = 6 + 216 18 − 0 = 6 + 12 = 18 lbs M = ∫ 0 6   λ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 6   1 + x 2 6 d x = x + x 3 18 0 6 = 6 + 216 18 − 0 = 6 + 12 = 18 lbs M=int_(0)^(6)lambda(x)dx=int_(0)^(6)(1+(x^(2))/(6))dx=[x+(x^(3))/(18)]_(0)^(6)=6+(216)/(18)-0=6+12=18lbsM=\int_{0}^{6} \lambda(x) d x=\int_{0}^{6}\left(1+\frac{x^{2}}{6}\right) d x=\left[x+\frac{x^{3}}{18}\right]_{0}^{6}=6+\frac{216}{18}-0=6+12=18 \mathrm{lbs}M=∫06λ(x)dx=∫06(1+x26)dx=[x+x318]06=6+21618−0=6+12=18lbs
Note: Don't forget to give the correct units at the end!

Example

A pyramid is 10 m tall and has a square base of side length 5 m . If the pyramid is made of a stone whose density is
ρ ( h ) = 10 − h 4 tonnes / m , ρ ( h ) = 10 − h 4  tonnes  / m , rho(h)=10-(h)/(4)" tonnes "//m,\rho(h)=10-\frac{h}{4} \text { tonnes } / \mathrm{m},ρ(h)=10−h4 tonnes /m,
where h h hhh is the height in metres above the base, find the mass of the pyramid.
Slice the pyramid horizontally. The slice is approximately a square slab of thickness Δ h Δ h Delta h\Delta hΔh and side length
s = 5 ( 1 − h 10 ) = 5 − h 2 m . s = 5 1 − h 10 = 5 − h 2 m . s=5(1-(h)/( 10))=5-(h)/(2)m.s=5\left(1-\frac{h}{10}\right)=5-\frac{h}{2} \mathrm{~m} .s=5(1−h10)=5−h2 m.
The mass of this slice is then approximately
s 2 Δ h ⋅ ρ ( h ) = ( 5 − h 2 ) 2 Δ h ( 10 − h 4 ) = ( 5 − h 2 ) 2 ⋅ 1 2 ( 20 − h 2 ) Δ h = 1 2 ( 5 − h 2 ) 2 ( 15 + ( 5 − h 2 ) ) Δ h . s 2 Δ h ⋅ ρ ( h ) = 5 − h 2 2 Δ h 10 − h 4 = 5 − h 2 2 ⋅ 1 2 20 − h 2 Δ h = 1 2 5 − h 2 2 15 + 5 − h 2 Δ h . {:[s^(2)Delta h*rho(h)=(5-(h)/(2))^(2)Delta h(10-(h)/(4))],[=(5-(h)/(2))^(2)*(1)/(2)(20-(h)/(2))Delta h=(1)/(2)(5-(h)/(2))^(2)(15+(5-(h)/(2)))Delta h.]:}\begin{gathered} s^{2} \Delta h \cdot \rho(h)=\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} \Delta h\left(10-\frac{h}{4}\right) \\ =\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(20-\frac{h}{2}\right) \Delta h=\frac{1}{2}\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}\left(15+\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)\right) \Delta h . \end{gathered}s2Δh⋅ρ(h)=(5−h2)2Δh(10−h4)=(5−h2)2⋅12(20−h2)Δh=12(5−h2)2(15+(5−h2))Δh.
Get the integral:
M = 1 2 ∫ 0 10 ( 5 − h 2 ) 2 ( 15 + ( 5 − h 2 ) ) d h M = 1 2 ∫ 0 10   5 − h 2 2 15 + 5 − h 2 d h M=(1)/(2)int_(0)^(10)(5-(h)/(2))^(2)(15+(5-(h)/(2)))dhM=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{10}\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}\left(15+\left(5-\frac{h}{2}\right)\right) d hM=12∫010(5−h2)2(15+(5−h2))dh
Let w = 5 − h 2 w = 5 − h 2 w=5-(h)/(2)w=5-\frac{h}{2}w=5−h2, so d w = − 1 2 d h d w = − 1 2 d h dw=-(1)/(2)dhd w=-\frac{1}{2} d hdw=−12dh.
When h = 0 , w = 5 h = 0 , w = 5 h=0,w=5h=0, w=5h=0,w=5; when h = 10 , w = 0 h = 10 , w = 0 h=10,w=0h=10, w=0h=10,w=0.
= 1 2 ∫ 5 0 w 2 ( 15 − w ) d w − 1 / 2 = 1 4 ∫ 0 5 ( 15 w 2 − w 3 ) d w = 1 4 [ 5 w 3 − w 4 4 ] 0 5 = 1 4 ( 625 − 625 4 − 0 ) ≈ 117 tonnes. = 1 2 ∫ 5 0   w 2 ( 15 − w ) d w − 1 / 2 = 1 4 ∫ 0 5   15 w 2 − w 3 d w = 1 4 5 w 3 − w 4 4 0 5 = 1 4 625 − 625 4 − 0 ≈ 117  tonnes.  {:[=(1)/(2)int_(5)^(0)w^(2)(15-w)(dw)/(-1//2)=(1)/(4)int_(0)^(5)(15w^(2)-w^(3))dw=(1)/(4)[5w^(3)-(w^(4))/(4)]_(0)^(5)],[=(1)/(4)(625-(625)/(4)-0)~~117" tonnes. "]:}\begin{gathered} =\frac{1}{2} \int_{5}^{0} w^{2}(15-w) \frac{d w}{-1 / 2}=\frac{1}{4} \int_{0}^{5}\left(15 w^{2}-w^{3}\right) d w=\frac{1}{4}\left[5 w^{3}-\frac{w^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{5} \\ =\frac{1}{4}\left(625-\frac{625}{4}-0\right) \approx 117 \text { tonnes. } \end{gathered}=12∫50w2(15−w)dw−1/2=14∫05(15w2−w3)dw=14[5w3−w44]05=14(625−6254−0)≈117 tonnes. 

Example

A flying ring is in the form of a thin annulus of inner radius 16 cm and outer radius 20 cm . If the area density of the ring as a function of radius is
σ ( r ) = 6 − ( r − 18 ) 2 10 grams / cm 2 σ ( r ) = 6 − ( r − 18 ) 2 10  grams  / cm 2 sigma(r)=6-((r-18)^(2))/(10)" grams "//cm^(2)\sigma(r)=6-\frac{(r-18)^{2}}{10} \text { grams } / \mathrm{cm}^{2}σ(r)=6−(r−18)210 grams /cm2
find the mass of the ring.

Here a small piece is a very thin ring of radius r r rrr and width Δ r Δ r Delta r\Delta rΔr. The area of this piece is approximately circumference × Δ r × Δ r xx Delta r\times \Delta r×Δr, i.e.
2 π r Δ r 2 π r Δ r 2pi r Delta r2 \pi r \Delta r2πrΔr
and the mass is approximately
Δ M = 2 π r σ ( r ) Δ r = 2 π r ( 6 − ( r − 18 ) 2 10 ) Δ r = 2 π r 10 ( 6 − ( r 2 − 36 r + 324 ) ) Δ r = 2 π r 10 ( 6 − r 2 + 36 r − 320 ) Δ r = 2 π r 10 ( − r 2 + 36 r − 318 ) Δ r = π 5 ( − r 3 + 36 r 2 − 318 r ) Δ r Δ M = 2 π r σ ( r ) Δ r = 2 π r 6 − ( r − 18 ) 2 10 Δ r = 2 π r 10 6 − r 2 − 36 r + 324 Δ r = 2 π r 10 6 − r 2 + 36 r − 320 Δ r = 2 π r 10 − r 2 + 36 r − 318 Δ r = π 5 − r 3 + 36 r 2 − 318 r Δ r {:[Delta M=2pi r sigma(r)Delta r=2pi r(6-((r-18)^(2))/(10))Delta r],[=(2pi r)/(10)(6-(r^(2)-36 r+324))Delta r=(2pi r)/(10)(6-r^(2)+36 r-320)Delta r],[=(2pi r)/(10)(-r^(2)+36 r-318)Delta r=(pi)/(5)(-r^(3)+36r^(2)-318 r)Delta r]:}\begin{gathered} \Delta M=2 \pi r \sigma(r) \Delta r=2 \pi r\left(6-\frac{(r-18)^{2}}{10}\right) \Delta r \\ =\frac{2 \pi r}{10}\left(6-\left(r^{2}-36 r+324\right)\right) \Delta r=\frac{2 \pi r}{10}\left(6-r^{2}+36 r-320\right) \Delta r \\ =\frac{2 \pi r}{10}\left(-r^{2}+36 r-318\right) \Delta r=\frac{\pi}{5}\left(-r^{3}+36 r^{2}-318 r\right) \Delta r \end{gathered}ΔM=2πrσ(r)Δr=2πr(6−(r−18)210)Δr=2πr10(6−(r2−36r+324))Δr=2πr10(6−r2+36r−320)Δr=2πr10(−r2+36r−318)Δr=π5(−r3+36r2−318r)Δr
The total mass is then approximately
M ≈ ∑ i = 0 n − 1 π 5 ( − r i − 1 3 + 36 r i − 1 2 − 318 r i − 1 ) Δ r M ≈ ∑ i = 0 n − 1   π 5 − r i − 1 3 + 36 r i − 1 2 − 318 r i − 1 Δ r M~~sum_(i=0)^(n-1)(pi)/(5)(-r_(i-1)^(3)+36r_(i-1)^(2)-318r_(i-1))Delta rM \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{\pi}{5}\left(-r_{i-1}^{3}+36 r_{i-1}^{2}-318 r_{i-1}\right) \Delta rM≈∑i=0n−1π5(−ri−13+36ri−12−318ri−1)Δr
and in the limit
M = π 5 ∫ 16 20 ( − r 3 + 36 r 2 − 318 r ) d r = π 5 [ − r 4 4 + 12 r 3 − 159 r 2 ] 16 20 = 67.2 π grams ≈ 211 grams. M = π 5 ∫ 16 20   − r 3 + 36 r 2 − 318 r d r = π 5 − r 4 4 + 12 r 3 − 159 r 2 16 20 = 67.2 π  grams  ≈ 211  grams.  {:[M=(pi)/(5)int_(16)^(20)(-r^(3)+36r^(2)-318 r)dr=(pi)/(5)[-(r^(4))/(4)+12r^(3)-159r^(2)]_(16)^(20)],[=67.2 pi" grams "~~211" grams. "]:}\begin{gathered} M=\frac{\pi}{5} \int_{16}^{20}\left(-r^{3}+36 r^{2}-318 r\right) d r=\frac{\pi}{5}\left[-\frac{r^{4}}{4}+12 r^{3}-159 r^{2}\right]_{16}^{20} \\ =67.2 \pi \text { grams } \approx 211 \text { grams. } \end{gathered}M=π5∫1620(−r3+36r2−318r)dr=π5[−r44+12r3−159r2]1620=67.2π grams ≈211 grams. 

Centre of Mass

A child of 50 kg and a child of 100 kg sit on a seesaw.
In order to balance, the heavy child needs to be twice as close to the fulcrum as the light child, e.g. if the light child is 2 m away from the fulcrum, then the heavy child must be 1 m away.
What is the same for both children is the product of the mass and the distance from the fulcrum, i.e. the moments about the fulcrum:
50 kg × 2 m = 100 kg × 1 m . 50 kg × 2 m = 100 kg × 1 m . 50kgxx2m=100kgxx1m.50 \mathrm{~kg} \times 2 \mathrm{~m}=100 \mathrm{~kg} \times 1 \mathrm{~m} .50 kg×2 m=100 kg×1 m.
Suppose now we have n n nnn masses m 1 , m 2 , … , m n m 1 , m 2 , … , m n m_(1),m_(2),dots,m_(n)m_{1}, m_{2}, \ldots, m_{n}m1,m2,…,mn in a line at positions x 1 , x 2 , … , x n x 1 , x 2 , … , x n x_(1),x_(2),dots,x_(n)x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}x1,x2,…,xn (respectively) on the x x xxx-axis.
We want to find where to put our fulcrum x ¯ x ¯ bar(x)\bar{x}x¯ so that the whole system balances.
For a given mass m i m i m_(i)m_{i}mi, the moment of this mass about x ¯ x ¯ bar(x)\bar{x}x¯ is
m i ( x i − x ¯ ) . m i x i − x ¯ . m_(i)(x_(i)-( bar(x))).m_{i}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right) .mi(xi−x¯).
N.B. this is positive if x i x i x_(i)x_{i}xi is to the right of x ¯ x ¯ bar(x)\bar{x}x¯ and negative if x i x i x_(i)x_{i}xi is to the left.
For the system to balance, the sum of all the moments about x ¯ x ¯ bar(x)\bar{x}x¯ must be zero:
∑ i = 1 n m i ( x i − x ¯ ) = 0 . ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i − x ¯ = 0 . sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)(x_(i)-( bar(x)))=0.\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)=0 .∑i=1nmi(xi−x¯)=0.
So
∑ i = 1 n m i x i − ∑ i = 1 n m i x ¯ = 0 ∑ i = 1 n m i x i − x ¯ ∑ i = 1 n m i = 0 ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i − ∑ i = 1 n   m i x ¯ = 0 ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i − x ¯ ∑ i = 1 n   m i = 0 {:[sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)x_(i)-sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i) bar(x)=0],[sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)x_(i)- bar(x)sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)=0]:}\begin{aligned} & \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} x_{i}-\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} \bar{x}=0 \\ & \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} x_{i}-\bar{x} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i}=0 \end{aligned}∑i=1nmixi−∑i=1nmix¯=0∑i=1nmixi−x¯∑i=1nmi=0
x ¯ ∑ i = 1 n m i = ∑ i = 1 n m i x i x ¯ = ∑ i = 1 n m i x i ∑ i = 1 n m i = ∑ i = 1 n m i x i M , x ¯ ∑ i = 1 n   m i = ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i x ¯ = ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i ∑ i = 1 n   m i = ∑ i = 1 n   m i x i M , {:[ bar(x)sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)=sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)x_(i)],[ bar(x)=(sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)x_(i))/(sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i))=(sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)x_(i))/(M)","]:}\begin{gathered} \bar{x} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} x_{i} \\ \bar{x}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} x_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i}}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} x_{i}}{M}, \end{gathered}x¯∑i=1nmi=∑i=1nmixix¯=∑i=1nmixi∑i=1nmi=∑i=1nmixiM,
where M = ∑ i = 1 n m i M = ∑ i = 1 n   m i M=sum_(i=1)^(n)m_(i)M=\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i}M=∑i=1nmi is the total mass.
Can think of x ¯ x ¯ bar(x)\bar{x}x¯ as the weighted average position of the whole system.

Example

The two children again.
Set m 1 = 50 kg , x 1 = 0 , m 2 = 100 kg , x 2 = 3 m 1 = 50 kg , x 1 = 0 , m 2 = 100 kg , x 2 = 3 m_(1)=50kg,x_(1)=0,quadm_(2)=100kg,x_(2)=3m_{1}=50 \mathrm{~kg}, x_{1}=0, \quad m_{2}=100 \mathrm{~kg}, x_{2}=3m1=50 kg,x1=0,m2=100 kg,x2=3.
x ¯ = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 m 1 + m 2 = 50 ( 0 ) + 100 ( 3 ) 50 + 100 = 300 150 = 2 m , x ¯ = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 m 1 + m 2 = 50 ( 0 ) + 100 ( 3 ) 50 + 100 = 300 150 = 2 m , bar(x)=(m_(1)x_(1)+m_(2)x_(2))/(m_(1)+m_(2))=(50(0)+100(3))/(50+100)=(300)/(150)=2m,\bar{x}=\frac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}=\frac{50(0)+100(3)}{50+100}=\frac{300}{150}=2 \mathrm{~m},x¯=m1x1+m2x2m1+m2=50(0)+100(3)50+100=300150=2 m,
as we'd expect.
For objects which cover entire regions and not just a number of points, these sums become integrals.
e.g. for an object of linear density λ ( x ) λ ( x ) lambda(x)\lambda(x)λ(x) which lies between x = a x = a x=ax=ax=a and x = b x = b x=bx=bx=b, we have
x ¯ = ∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x ∫ a b λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x M x ¯ = ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x ∫ a b   λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x M bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx)/(int_(a)^(b)lambda(x)dx)=(int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx)/(M)\bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x}{\int_{a}^{b} \lambda(x) d x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x}{M}x¯=∫abxλ(x)dx∫abλ(x)dx=∫abxλ(x)dxM

Example

For a uniform rod of constant density λ λ lambda\lambdaλ which extends from x = a x = a x=ax=ax=a to x = b x = b x=bx=bx=b, the centre of mass is found by getting
∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b x λ d x = [ λ x 2 2 ] a b = λ 2 ( b 2 − a 2 ) ∫ a b λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b λ d x = [ λ x ] a b = λ ( b − a ) ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b   x λ d x = λ x 2 2 a b = λ 2 b 2 − a 2 ∫ a b   λ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b   λ d x = [ λ x ] a b = λ ( b − a ) {:[int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx=int_(a)^(b)x lambda dx=[(lambdax^(2))/(2)]_(a)^(b)=(lambda)/(2)(b^(2)-a^(2))],[int_(a)^(b)lambda(x)dx=int_(a)^(b)lambda dx=[lambda x]_(a)^(b)=lambda(b-a)]:}\begin{gathered} \int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} x \lambda d x=\left[\frac{\lambda x^{2}}{2}\right]_{a}^{b}=\frac{\lambda}{2}\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right) \\ \int_{a}^{b} \lambda(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} \lambda d x=[\lambda x]_{a}^{b}=\lambda(b-a) \end{gathered}∫abxλ(x)dx=∫abxλdx=[λx22]ab=λ2(b2−a2)∫abλ(x)dx=∫abλdx=[λx]ab=λ(b−a)
So
x ¯ = ∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x ∫ a b λ ( x ) d x = λ 2 ( b 2 − a 2 ) λ ( b − a ) = 1 2 ( b − a ) ( b + a ) b − a = b + a 2 x ¯ = ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x ∫ a b   λ ( x ) d x = λ 2 b 2 − a 2 λ ( b − a ) = 1 2 ( b − a ) ( b + a ) b − a = b + a 2 bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx)/(int_(a)^(b)lambda(x)dx)=((lambda)/(2)(b^(2)-a^(2)))/(lambda(b-a))=(1)/(2)((b-a)(b+a))/(b-a)=(b+a)/(2)\bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x}{\int_{a}^{b} \lambda(x) d x}=\frac{\frac{\lambda}{2}\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right)}{\lambda(b-a)}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(b-a)(b+a)}{b-a}=\frac{b+a}{2}x¯=∫abxλ(x)dx∫abλ(x)dx=λ2(b2−a2)λ(b−a)=12(b−a)(b+a)b−a=b+a2
as we'd expect.

Example

The 6 ft rod of linear density
λ ( x ) = 1 + x 2 6 lb / ft λ ( x ) = 1 + x 2 6 lb / ft lambda(x)=1+(x^(2))/(6)lb//ft\lambda(x)=1+\frac{x^{2}}{6} \mathrm{lb} / \mathrm{ft}λ(x)=1+x26lb/ft
of last time.
Already found that M = 18 lbs M = 18 lbs M=18lbsM=18 \mathrm{lbs}M=18lbs.
Now
∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 6 x ( 1 + x 2 6 ) d x = ∫ 0 6 ( x + x 3 6 ) d x = [ x 2 2 + x 4 24 ] 0 6 = 6 2 2 + 6 4 24 − 0 = 18 + 54 = 72 ft ⋅ lbs ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 6   x 1 + x 2 6 d x = ∫ 0 6   x + x 3 6 d x = x 2 2 + x 4 24 0 6 = 6 2 2 + 6 4 24 − 0 = 18 + 54 = 72 ft ⋅ lbs {:[int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx=int_(0)^(6)x(1+(x^(2))/(6))dx=int_(0)^(6)(x+(x^(3))/(6))dx],[=[(x^(2))/(2)+(x^(4))/(24)]_(0)^(6)=(6^(2))/(2)+(6^(4))/(24)-0=18+54=72ft*lbs]:}\begin{aligned} & \int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x=\int_{0}^{6} x\left(1+\frac{x^{2}}{6}\right) d x=\int_{0}^{6}\left(x+\frac{x^{3}}{6}\right) d x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{4}}{24}\right]_{0}^{6}=\frac{6^{2}}{2}+\frac{6^{4}}{24}-0=18+54=72 \mathrm{ft} \cdot \mathrm{lbs} \end{aligned}∫abxλ(x)dx=∫06x(1+x26)dx=∫06(x+x36)dx=[x22+x424]06=622+6424−0=18+54=72ft⋅lbs
Thus
x ¯ = ∫ a b x λ ( x ) d x M = 72 ft ⋅ lbs 18 lbs = 4 ft x ¯ = ∫ a b   x λ ( x ) d x M = 72 ft ⋅ lbs 18 lbs = 4 ft bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x lambda(x)dx)/(M)=(72ft*lbs)/(18lbs)=4ft\bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \lambda(x) d x}{M}=\frac{72 \mathrm{ft} \cdot \mathrm{lbs}}{18 \mathrm{lbs}}=4 \mathrm{ft}x¯=∫abxλ(x)dxM=72ft⋅lbs18lbs=4ft
For a 2D region lying between x = a x = a x=ax=ax=a and x = b x = b x=bx=bx=b and y = c y = c y=cy=cy=c and y = d y = d y=dy=dy=d, the coordinates of the centre of mass are given by
x ¯ = ∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x ∫ a b δ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x M y ¯ = ∫ c d y δ ( y ) d y ∫ c d δ ( y ) d y = ∫ c d y δ ( y ) d y M x ¯ = ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x ∫ a b   δ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x M y ¯ = ∫ c d   y δ ( y ) d y ∫ c d   δ ( y ) d y = ∫ c d   y δ ( y ) d y M {:[ bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx)/(int_(a)^(b)delta(x)dx)=(int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx)/(M)],[ bar(y)=(int_(c)^(d)y delta(y)dy)/(int_(c)^(d)delta(y)dy)=(int_(c)^(d)y delta(y)dy)/(M)]:}\begin{aligned} & \bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x}{\int_{a}^{b} \delta(x) d x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x}{M} \\ & \bar{y}=\frac{\int_{c}^{d} y \delta(y) d y}{\int_{c}^{d} \delta(y) d y}=\frac{\int_{c}^{d} y \delta(y) d y}{M} \end{aligned}x¯=∫abxδ(x)dx∫abδ(x)dx=∫abxδ(x)dxMy¯=∫cdyδ(y)dy∫cdδ(y)dy=∫cdyδ(y)dyM
Here δ ( x ) , δ ( y ) δ ( x ) , δ ( y ) delta(x),delta(y)\delta(x), \delta(y)δ(x),δ(y) refer to the mass per unit length in the x x xxx - and y y yyy-directions respectively.
An important special case is when we have a uniform region with constant density σ σ sigma\sigmaσ :
x ¯ = σ ∫ a b x h ( x ) d x M , y ¯ = σ ∫ c d y w ( y ) d y M x ¯ = σ ∫ a b   x h ( x ) d x M , y ¯ = σ ∫ c d   y w ( y ) d y M bar(x)=(sigmaint_(a)^(b)xh(x)dx)/(M),quad bar(y)=(sigmaint_(c)^(d)yw(y)dy)/(M)\bar{x}=\frac{\sigma \int_{a}^{b} x h(x) d x}{M}, \quad \bar{y}=\frac{\sigma \int_{c}^{d} y w(y) d y}{M}x¯=σ∫abxh(x)dxM,y¯=σ∫cdyw(y)dyM
Here h ( x ) h ( x ) h(x)h(x)h(x) is the height of a vertical slice perpendicular to the x x xxx-axis, and w ( y ) w ( y ) w(y)w(y)w(y) the width of a horizontal slice perpendicular to the y y yyy-axis.
Of course, here
M = ρ A M = ρ A M=rho AM=\rho AM=ρA
where A A AAA is the area and
A = ∫ a b h ( x ) d x = ∫ c d w ( y ) d y A = ∫ a b   h ( x ) d x = ∫ c d   w ( y ) d y A=int_(a)^(b)h(x)dx=int_(c)^(d)w(y)dyA=\int_{a}^{b} h(x) d x=\int_{c}^{d} w(y) d yA=∫abh(x)dx=∫cdw(y)dy

Example

Calculate the centre of mass of the uniform triangular lamina as shown.
Let σ σ sigma\sigmaσ be the constant area density.
We can find the mass by "cheating" since the lamina is uniform:
M = σ ( area ) = σ ⋅ 1 2 ⋅ 1 ⋅ 1 = σ 2 . M = σ (  area  ) = σ ⋅ 1 2 ⋅ 1 ⋅ 1 = σ 2 . M=sigma(" area ")=sigma*(1)/(2)*1*1=(sigma)/(2).M=\sigma(\text { area })=\sigma \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot 1=\frac{\sigma}{2} .M=σ( area )=σ⋅12⋅1⋅1=σ2.
For x ¯ , δ ( x ) x ¯ , δ ( x ) bar(x),delta(x)\bar{x}, \delta(x)x¯,δ(x) is given by
δ ( x ) = σ × length of small strip ≈ σ x . δ ( x ) = σ ×  length of small strip  ≈ σ x . delta(x)=sigma xx" length of small strip "~~sigma x.\delta(x)=\sigma \times \text { length of small strip } \approx \sigma x .δ(x)=σ× length of small strip ≈σx.
Thus
∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 1 x ⋅ σ x d x = σ ∫ 0 1 x 2 d x = σ [ x 3 3 ] 0 1 = σ 3 ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 1   x ⋅ σ x d x = σ ∫ 0 1   x 2 d x = σ x 3 3 0 1 = σ 3 int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx=int_(0)^(1)x*sigma xdx=sigmaint_(0)^(1)x^(2)dx=sigma[(x^(3))/(3)]_(0)^(1)=(sigma)/(3)\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x=\int_{0}^{1} x \cdot \sigma x d x=\sigma \int_{0}^{1} x^{2} d x=\sigma\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{\sigma}{3}∫abxδ(x)dx=∫01x⋅σxdx=σ∫01x2dx=σ[x33]01=σ3
For y ¯ , δ ( y ) y ¯ , δ ( y ) bar(y),delta(y)\bar{y}, \delta(y)y¯,δ(y) is given by
δ ( y ) = σ × length of small strip ≈ σ ( 1 − y ) . δ ( y ) = σ ×  length of small strip  ≈ σ ( 1 − y ) . delta(y)=sigma xx" length of small strip "~~sigma(1-y).\delta(y)=\sigma \times \text { length of small strip } \approx \sigma(1-y) .δ(y)=σ× length of small strip ≈σ(1−y).
Thus
∫ a b y δ ( y ) d y = ∫ 0 1 y σ ( 1 − y ) d y = σ ∫ 0 1 ( y − y 2 ) d y = σ [ y 2 2 − y 3 3 ] 0 1 = σ ( 1 2 − 1 3 − 0 ) = σ 6 ∫ a b   y δ ( y ) d y = ∫ 0 1   y σ ( 1 − y ) d y = σ ∫ 0 1   y − y 2 d y = σ y 2 2 − y 3 3 0 1 = σ 1 2 − 1 3 − 0 = σ 6 {:[int_(a)^(b)y delta(y)dy=int_(0)^(1)y sigma(1-y)dy=sigmaint_(0)^(1)(y-y^(2))dy],[=sigma[(y^(2))/(2)-(y^(3))/(3)]_(0)^(1)=sigma((1)/(2)-(1)/(3)-0)=(sigma)/(6)]:}\begin{gathered} \int_{a}^{b} y \delta(y) d y=\int_{0}^{1} y \sigma(1-y) d y=\sigma \int_{0}^{1}\left(y-y^{2}\right) d y \\ =\sigma\left[\frac{y^{2}}{2}-\frac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1}=\sigma\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}-0\right)=\frac{\sigma}{6} \end{gathered}∫abyδ(y)dy=∫01yσ(1−y)dy=σ∫01(y−y2)dy=σ[y22−y33]01=σ(12−13−0)=σ6
Finally,
x ¯ = ∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x M = σ / 3 σ / 2 = 2 3 , y ¯ = ∫ a b y δ ( y ) d y M = σ / 6 σ / 2 = 1 3 x ¯ = ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x M = σ / 3 σ / 2 = 2 3 , y ¯ = ∫ a b   y δ ( y ) d y M = σ / 6 σ / 2 = 1 3 bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx)/(M)=(sigma//3)/(sigma//2)=(2)/(3),quad bar(y)=(int_(a)^(b)y delta(y)dy)/(M)=(sigma//6)/(sigma//2)=(1)/(3)\bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x}{M}=\frac{\sigma / 3}{\sigma / 2}=\frac{2}{3}, \quad \bar{y}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} y \delta(y) d y}{M}=\frac{\sigma / 6}{\sigma / 2}=\frac{1}{3}x¯=∫abxδ(x)dxM=σ/3σ/2=23,y¯=∫abyδ(y)dyM=σ/6σ/2=13

Three-Dimensional Regions

For a 3-D region which lies between
a ≤ x ≤ b , c ≤ y ≤ d , e ≤ z ≤ f a ≤ x ≤ b , c ≤ y ≤ d , e ≤ z ≤ f a <= x <= b,quad c <= y <= d,quad e <= z <= fa \leq x \leq b, \quad c \leq y \leq d, \quad e \leq z \leq fa≤x≤b,c≤y≤d,e≤z≤f
the centre of mass is given by
x ¯ = ∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x ∫ a b δ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b x δ ( x ) d x M y ¯ = ∫ c d y δ ( y ) d y ∫ c d δ ( y ) d y = ∫ c d y δ ( y ) d y M z ¯ = ∫ e f z δ ( z ) d z ∫ e f δ ( z ) d z = ∫ e f z δ ( z ) d z M x ¯ = ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x ∫ a b   δ ( x ) d x = ∫ a b   x δ ( x ) d x M y ¯ = ∫ c d   y δ ( y ) d y ∫ c d   δ ( y ) d y = ∫ c d   y δ ( y ) d y M z ¯ = ∫ e f   z δ ( z ) d z ∫ e f   δ ( z ) d z = ∫ e f   z δ ( z ) d z M {:[ bar(x)=(int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx)/(int_(a)^(b)delta(x)dx)=(int_(a)^(b)x delta(x)dx)/(M)],[ bar(y)=(int_(c)^(d)y delta(y)dy)/(int_(c)^(d)delta(y)dy)=(int_(c)^(d)y delta(y)dy)/(M)],[ bar(z)=(int_(e)^(f)z delta(z)dz)/(int_(e)^(f)delta(z)dz)=(int_(e)^(f)z delta(z)dz)/(M)]:}\begin{aligned} & \bar{x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x}{\int_{a}^{b} \delta(x) d x}=\frac{\int_{a}^{b} x \delta(x) d x}{M} \\ & \bar{y}=\frac{\int_{c}^{d} y \delta(y) d y}{\int_{c}^{d} \delta(y) d y}=\frac{\int_{c}^{d} y \delta(y) d y}{M} \\ & \bar{z}=\frac{\int_{e}^{f} z \delta(z) d z}{\int_{e}^{f} \delta(z) d z}=\frac{\int_{e}^{f} z \delta(z) d z}{M} \end{aligned}x¯=∫abxδ(x)dx∫abδ(x)dx=∫abxδ(x)dxMy¯=∫cdyδ(y)dy∫cdδ(y)dy=∫cdyδ(y)dyMz¯=∫efzδ(z)dz∫efδ(z)dz=∫efzδ(z)dzM
Where δ ( x ) , δ ( y ) , δ ( z ) δ ( x ) , δ ( y ) , δ ( z ) delta(x),delta(y),delta(z)\delta(x), \delta(y), \delta(z)δ(x),δ(y),δ(z) are the mass per unit length in the x x xxx-, y y yyy - and z z zzz-directions.
An important special case is when we have a constant uniform density ρ ρ rho\rhoρ. In this case
x ¯ = ρ ∫ a b x A ( x ) d x M , y ¯ = ρ ∫ c d y A ( y ) d y M , z ¯ = ρ ∫ e f z A ( z ) d z M x ¯ = ρ ∫ a b   x A ( x ) d x M , y ¯ = ρ ∫ c d   y A ( y ) d y M , z ¯ = ρ ∫ e f   z A ( z ) d z M bar(x)=(rhoint_(a)^(b)xA(x)dx)/(M),quad bar(y)=(rhoint_(c)^(d)yA(y)dy)/(M),quad bar(z)=(rhoint_(e)^(f)zA(z)dz)/(M)\bar{x}=\frac{\rho \int_{a}^{b} x A(x) d x}{M}, \quad \bar{y}=\frac{\rho \int_{c}^{d} y A(y) d y}{M}, \quad \bar{z}=\frac{\rho \int_{e}^{f} z A(z) d z}{M}x¯=ρ∫abxA(x)dxM,y¯=ρ∫cdyA(y)dyM,z¯=ρ∫efzA(z)dzM
where A ( x ) , A ( y ) , A ( z ) A ( x ) , A ( y ) , A ( z ) A(x),A(y),A(z)A(x), A(y), A(z)A(x),A(y),A(z) are the cross-sectional areas obtained by slicing perpendicular to the x x xxx-, y y yyy and z z zzz-axes.

Example

A uniform hemisphere of radius r r rrr.
If we place the hemisphere as shown, then by symmetry x ¯ = y ¯ = 0 x ¯ = y ¯ = 0 bar(x)= bar(y)=0\bar{x}=\bar{y}=0x¯=y¯=0, so we only need to find z ¯ z ¯ bar(z)\bar{z}z¯.
Again, since the density is uniform, it is easy to find the mass:
mass = density × volume = ρ ⋅ 2 π r 3 3 = 2 π r 3 ρ 3 .  mass  =  density × volume  = ρ ⋅ 2 π r 3 3 = 2 π r 3 ρ 3 . " mass "=" density × volume "=rho*(2pir^(3))/(3)=(2pir^(3)rho)/(3).\text { mass }=\text { density × volume }=\rho \cdot \frac{2 \pi r^{3}}{3}=\frac{2 \pi r^{3} \rho}{3} . mass = density × volume =ρ⋅2πr33=2πr3ρ3.
So we only need to find
ρ ∫ e f z A ( z ) d z ρ ∫ e f   z A ( z ) d z rhoint_(e)^(f)zA(z)dz\rho \int_{e}^{f} z A(z) d zρ∫efzA(z)dz
The cross-section at height z z zzz is a disc of radius s ( z ) s ( z ) s(z)s(z)s(z) where
s ( z ) = r 2 − z 2 s ( z ) = r 2 − z 2 s(z)=sqrt(r^(2)-z^(2))s(z)=\sqrt{r^{2}-z^{2}}s(z)=r2−z2
The area of this disc is then
A ( z ) = π ( s ( z ) ) 2 = π ( r 2 − z 2 ) 2 = π ( r 2 − z 2 ) A ( z ) = π ( s ( z ) ) 2 = π r 2 − z 2 2 = π r 2 − z 2 A(z)=pi(s(z))^(2)=pi(sqrt(r^(2)-z^(2)))^(2)=pi(r^(2)-z^(2))A(z)=\pi(s(z))^{2}=\pi\left(\sqrt{r^{2}-z^{2}}\right)^{2}=\pi\left(r^{2}-z^{2}\right)A(z)=π(s(z))2=π(r2−z2)2=π(r2−z2)
Thus
ρ ∫ e f z A ( z ) d z = ρ ∫ 0 r z π ( r 2 − z 2 ) d z = π ρ ∫ 0 r ( r 2 z − z 3 ) d z = π ρ [ r 2 z 2 2 − z 4 4 ] 0 r = π ρ ( r 4 2 − r 4 4 − 0 ) = π r 4 ρ 4 ρ ∫ e f   z A ( z ) d z = ρ ∫ 0 r   z π r 2 − z 2 d z = π ρ ∫ 0 r   r 2 z − z 3 d z = π ρ r 2 z 2 2 − z 4 4 0 r = π ρ r 4 2 − r 4 4 − 0 = π r 4 ρ 4 {:[rhoint_(e)^(f)zA(z)dz=rhoint_(0)^(r)z pi(r^(2)-z^(2))dz=pi rhoint_(0)^(r)(r^(2)z-z^(3))dz],[quad=pi rho[(r^(2)z^(2))/(2)-(z^(4))/(4)]_(0)^(r)=pi rho((r^(4))/(2)-(r^(4))/(4)-0)=(pir^(4)rho)/(4)]:}\begin{gathered} \rho \int_{e}^{f} z A(z) d z=\rho \int_{0}^{r} z \pi\left(r^{2}-z^{2}\right) d z=\pi \rho \int_{0}^{r}\left(r^{2} z-z^{3}\right) d z \\ \quad=\pi \rho\left[\frac{r^{2} z^{2}}{2}-\frac{z^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{r}=\pi \rho\left(\frac{r^{4}}{2}-\frac{r^{4}}{4}-0\right)=\frac{\pi r^{4} \rho}{4} \end{gathered}ρ∫efzA(z)dz=ρ∫0rzπ(r2−z2)dz=πρ∫0r(r2z−z3)dz=πρ[r2z22−z44]0r=πρ(r42−r44−0)=πr4ρ4
Finally
z ¯ = ∫ e f z A ( z ) d z M = π r 4 ρ / 4 2 π r 3 ρ / 3 = r 4 / 4 ( 2 / 3 ) r 3 = r ⋅ 1 4 ⋅ 3 2 = 3 r 8 z ¯ = ∫ e f   z A ( z ) d z M = π r 4 ρ / 4 2 π r 3 ρ / 3 = r 4 / 4 ( 2 / 3 ) r 3 = r ⋅ 1 4 ⋅ 3 2 = 3 r 8 bar(z)=(int_(e)^(f)zA(z)dz)/(M)=(pir^(4)rho//4)/(2pir^(3)rho//3)=(r^(4)//4)/((2//3)r^(3))=r*(1)/(4)*(3)/(2)=(3r)/(8)\bar{z}=\frac{\int_{e}^{f} z A(z) d z}{M}=\frac{\pi r^{4} \rho / 4}{2 \pi r^{3} \rho / 3}=\frac{r^{4} / 4}{(2 / 3) r^{3}}=r \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{3}{2}=\frac{3 r}{8}z¯=∫efzA(z)dzM=πr4ρ/42πr3ρ/3=r4/4(2/3)r3=r⋅14⋅32=3r8
Questions. Does this answer have the right units? Does it surprise you that ρ ρ rho\rhoρ does not appear in the answer? Is the answer about the size you'd expect?
